text:
Git branching is an essential part of modern version control workflows. When working on a project, developers create feature branches to build new functionality without affecting the main codebase. This keeps the primary branch stable and ready for deployment.

Once work on a feature branch is complete, the developer submits a pull request. This invites team members to review the changes, discuss potential issues, and approve the work before it is merged into the main branch. Merging combines the changes from the feature branch, integrating the new code with the rest of the project.

This workflow helps teams collaborate efficiently, avoid conflicts, and maintain a clear history of changes. Understanding how to create branches, handle pull requests, and perform merges is a fundamental skill for any developer.
